perturbation.original_text: The Bank has established compliance programs and is in compliance with the requirements of the USA Patriot Act and all applicable regulations promulgated thereunder. The Bank is in compliance with the USA Patriot Act and all applicable regulations promulgated thereunder, and there is no charge, investigation, action, suit or proceeding before any court, regulatory authority or governmental agency or body pending or, to the best knowledge of the Company, the MHCs, and the Bank, threatened regarding the Bank's compliance with the USA Patriot Act or any regulations promulgated thereunder.
perturbation.changed_text: The Bank has established compliance programs, and there is no charge, investigation, action, suit or proceeding before any court, regulatory authority or governmental agency or body pending or, to the best knowledge of the Company, the MHCs, and the Bank, threatened regarding the Bank's compliance with the USA Patriot Act or any regulations promulgated thereunder.
perturbation.explanation: This change removes the explicit statement that the Bank 'is in compliance with the USA Patriot Act and all applicable regulations promulgated thereunder'. By omitting this, it obscures the Bank's obligations and could potentially mislead investors, thereby creating a compliance risk.
perturbation.law_explanation: 31 U.S.C. § 5318(h) mandates financial institutions to establish anti-money laundering programs, including compliance procedures. By removing the explicit statement of compliance, the modified text contradicts the implicit assurance that the bank adheres to these mandatory AML obligations, creating legal risk as it suggests potential non-compliance.

Compliance, exemptions, and summons authority (a) General Powers of Secretary .-The Secretary of the Treasury may (except under section 5315 of this title and regulations prescribed under section 5315)- (1) except as provided in subsections (b)(2) and (h)(4), delegate duti
perturbation.justification: The original text explicitly states the Bank's compliance with the USA Patriot Act and its regulations, while the changed text omits this statement, only mentioning the establishment of compliance programs and absence of pending legal actions. According to 31 U.S.C. § 5318(h), financial institutions are mandated to establish anti-money laundering programs, including compliance procedures. By removing the explicit statement of compliance, the modified text contradicts the implicit assurance that the bank adheres to these mandatory AML obligations.

perturbation.original_text: Each of the Company, the MHCs, the Bank and each of the Subsidiaries maintains a system of internal accounting controls sufficient to provide reasonable assurance that (a) transactions are executed in accordance with management's general or specific authorizations; (b) transactions are recorded as necessary to permit preparation of financial statements in conformity with generally accepted accounting principles and to maintain asset accountability; (c) access to assets is permitted only in accordance with management's general or specific authorization; and (d) the recorded accountability for assets is compared with the existing assets at reasonable intervals and appropriate action is taken with respect to any differences.
perturbation.changed_text: Each of the Company, the MHCs, the Bank and each of the Subsidiaries maintains a system of internal accounting controls sufficient to provide some assurance that (a) transactions are executed in accordance with some form of authorizations; (b) transactions are recorded to permit preparation of financial statements and to maintain some level of asset accountability; (c) access to assets is sometimes permitted; and (d) the recorded accountability for assets is occasionally compared with the existing assets.
perturbation.explanation: This change weakens the assertion regarding internal accounting controls. The original language implies robust controls providing 'reasonable assurance,' executed with 'general or specific authorization,' recorded to conform to 'generally accepted accounting principles,' and compared at 'reasonable intervals.' The modified language weakens these requirements, suggesting a weaker control environment which may not comply with legal requirements for financial institutions. 'Some,' 'some form,' 'some level,' 'sometimes,' and 'occasionally' all significantly weaken the requirements.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 12 U.S.C. § 1831o - Early detection of needed improvements in financial management
perturbation.law_explanation: 12 U.S.C. § 1831o mandates that federal banking agencies prescribe standards for internal controls and information systems for insured depository institutions. By weakening the description of the internal accounting controls, the modified text suggests a potential failure to meet the required standards for early detection of financial management issues, contradicting the purpose and requirements of the law.

Prompt corrective action (a) Resolving problems to protect Deposit Insurance Fund (1) Purpose The purpose of this section is to resolve the problems of insured depository institutions at the least possible long-term loss to the Deposit Insurance Fund. (2) Prompt corrective action required Each appropriate Federal banking agency and the Corporation (acting in the Corporation's capacity as the insurer of depository institutions under this chapter) shall carry out the purpose of this section by taking prompt corrective action to resolve the problems of insured depository institutions. (b) Definitions For purposes of this section: (1) Capital categories (A) Well capitalized An insured depository institution is "well capitalized" if it significantly exceeds the required minimum level for each relevant capital measure. (B) Adequately capitalized An insured depository institution is "adequately capitalized" if it meets the required minimum level for each relevant capital measure. (C) Undercapitalized An insured depository institution is "undercapitalized" if it fails to meet the required minimum level for any relevant capital measure. (D) Significantly undercapitalized An insured depository institution is "significantly undercapitalized" if it is significantly below the required minimum level for any relevant capital measure. (E) Critically undercapitalized An insured depository institution is "critically undercapitalized" if it fails to meet any level specified under subsection (c)(3)(A). (2) Other definitions (A) Average (i) In general The "average" of an accounting item (such as total assets or tangible equity) during a given period means the sum of that item at the close of business on each business day during that period divided by the total number of business days in that period. (ii) Agency may permit weekly averaging for certain institutions In the case of insured depository institutions that have total assets of less than $300,000,000 and normally file report

Prompt corrective action (a) Resolving problems to protect Deposit Insurance Fund (1) Purpose The purpose of this section is to resolve the problems of insured depository institutions at the least possible long-term loss to the Deposit Insurance Fund. (2) Prompt corrective action required Each appropriate Federal banking agency and the Corporation (acting in the Corporation's capacity as the insurer of depository institutions under this chapter) shall carry out the purpose of this section by taking prompt corrective action to resolve the problems of insured depository institutions. (b) Definitions For purposes of this section: (1) Capital categories (A) Well capitalized An insured depository institution is “well capitalized” if it significantly exceeds the required minimum level for each relevant capital measure. (B) Adequately capitalized An insured depository institution is “adequately capitalized” if it meets the required minimum level for each relevant capital measure. (C) Undercapitalized An insured depository institution is “undercapitalized” if it fails to meet the required minimum level for any relevant capital measure. (D) Significantly undercapitalized An insured depository institution is “significantly undercapitalized” if it is significantly below the required minimum level for any relevant capital measure. (E) Critically undercapitalized An insured depository institution is “critically undercapitalized” if it fails to meet any level specified under subsection (c)(3)(A) of this section. (ii) Agency may permit weekly averaging for certain institutions In the case of insured depository institutions that have total assets of less than $300,000,000 and norm
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces several weakening terms like 'some assurance,' 'some form,' 'some level,' 'sometimes,' and 'occasionally.' This directly undermines the original intent of maintaining a robust system of internal accounting controls. The Law Explanation states that 12 U.S.C. § 1831o mandates federal banking agencies to prescribe standards for internal controls and information systems for insured depository institutions. The scraped snippets, while primarily addressing 'Prompt corrective action,' implicitly support the need for well-defined standards, as the purpose of the law is 'to resolve the problems of insured depository institutions at the least possible long-term loss to the Deposit Insurance Fund.' By weakening the control requirements, the modified text creates a potential failure to meet the required standards, thus contradicting the purpose and implied requirements of 12 U.S.C. § 1831o.

perturbation.original_text: The Company will make generally available to its security holders as soon as practicable, but not later than 60 days after the close of the period covered thereby, an earnings statement covering a twelve month period beginning not later than the first day of the Company's fiscal quarter next following the effective date of the Registration Statement (as defined in Rule 158 of the Securities Act Regulations) that will satisfy the provisions of Section 11(a) of the Securities Act.
perturbation.changed_text: The Company will attempt to make available to its security holders an earnings statement covering a twelve month period. There is no guarantee that this earnings statement will satisfy the provisions of Section 11(a) of the Securities Act.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces ambiguity and removes the commitment to comply with Section 11(a) of the Securities Act. The original text mandates making the earnings statement available within 60 days, satisfying Section 11(a). The modified text weakens the commitment to 'attempt' and removes the assurance of satisfying Section 11(a), creating a legal risk related to investor information and potential liability.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 15 U.S. Code § 77k - Civil liabilities on account of false registration statement
perturbation.law_explanation: 15 U.S.C. § 77k imposes civil liabilities for false registration statements. By removing the guarantee that the earnings statement will comply with Section 11(a) (which dictates liability for misleading statements in registration statements), the modified text increases the potential for liability under this section, as it indicates a lack of commitment to providing accurate and complete information, thus contradicting the investor protection requirements of the law.

Civil liabilities on account of false registration statement (a) Persons possessing cause of action; persons liable In case any part of the registration statement, when such part became effective, contained an untrue statement of a material fact or omitted to state a material fact required to be stated therein or necessary to make the state
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the guarantee that the earnings statement will comply with Section 11(a) of the Securities Act, directly contradicting the investor protection requirements of 15 U.S.C. § 77k. The original text mandated compliance, while the modified text introduces uncertainty. Scraped Law Snippet 1 explicitly mentions "Civil liabilities on account of false registration statement," indicating that Section 11(a) compliance is crucial to avoid liability. The changed text increases the potential for false registration, thus contradicting the law.
